other_observers: Susan Allerton, Amy and Jo Dobbins, John Bregar, John and Jan Rees, Heather Morris, Leland Flores; Yes, I initially found and identified the bird.
duration: Off and on for three hours
specific_location: South end of Durango in trees along Animas River Trail. Bird was located directly behind/below Four Corners Riversports just east of Highway 550/160. Access on frontage road half-mile south of Santa Rita Drive.
description: The olive yellow crown was the first feature to catch my eye, followed by the yellow "slash" along the wing. The bird had a bluish/gray color on the upperparts and very light gray underparts. The bill seemed thin and almost needle-like. The head of the bird had a darker gray cheek patch with a white stripe above and a white malar stripe below. When I later consulted field guides, this bird seemed a bit more dull in overall color than the illustrations.Behaviors: This bird was constantly feeding and always flitting from tree to tree. Often the bird was upside down while feeding, hanging from a clump of leaves or buds. It often seemed to favor clumps of dead leaves in its feeding efforts. At times it was probably 60 feet off the ground and at other times it was within five feet of the ground. I also observed two strange interactions in which hummingbirds seemed to approach this bird and then be driven away.Call: thin, short, high chip given mostly in flightPlumage: breeding
